title: Beeswax votive candles
subtitle: Small beeswax candles in salve tins or votive moulds with lavender essential oil
excerpt: Small beeswax pours in metal tins with a pre-waxed wick. Beeswax burns cleaner and longer than paraffin per gram; lavender essential oil is added at a low 2% concentration to complement rather than compete with the natural honey note of the wax.
sourceNotes: Beeswax burn characteristics: standard candlemaking reference. Lavender oil stability in beeswax at low concentration: candle formulation practice.

recipe.yieldDescription: 3 to 4 small votive candles (about 30 g each)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Use the same beeswax-to-essential-oil ratio: 2% by wax weight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Beeswax votives last years in storage. Make batches and give as gifts.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Beeswax candles keep five or more years. Bloom (white surface film) is natural and normal.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cotton wick with a small metal sustainer tab at the base. The tab holds the wick to the bottom of the container while the wax sets. Available in different thicknesses (CD series) matched to jar diameter.

content.text: Small votive candles in metal tins are the simplest candle project: no jar to align the wick with, no risk of a large glass vessel cracking, and a quick 60-minute set time. Beeswax is ideal at this small scale because its high density means a 30 g tin burns for several hours.
make centring straightforward -- press the tab to the warm base before pouring.

items.prepNote: fills three to four 30 ml salve tins
use a thin wick -- CD-6 or CDN-12 -- for small containers

content.text: Warm the salve tins slightly (60°C oven, 3 to 5 minutes) to prevent the beeswax cracking on contact with a cold base. Press one wick tab firmly to the centre of each tin base.
Melt 120 g of beeswax pellets in the pouring pitcher over a double boiler to 75 to 80°C. Remove from the heat and cool to 65 to 70°C. Add the lavender essential oil if using and stir gently.

Pour the beeswax steadily into the tins, filling to about 5 mm below the rim. Hold each wick upright while the surface firms up -- beeswax sets faster than soy and you have about 5 to 10 minutes per tin. Leave to cool fully for at least one hour.

Trim the wick to 5 mm before lighting. Place on a heat-proof surface when burning -- the tin base will become hot. Never leave unattended. The small size means a full melt pool forms quickly; burn for no more than one to two hours per session.
